section.title: The Ark Returned
section.sectionRationale: Desperate to be rid of the ark, the Philistines devise a test: they send it home on a new cart pulled by two nursing cows, with a guilt offering of gold. The cows, against all instinct, go straight to Israel, proving it was the LORD's hand all along. Yet the joy at Beth-shemesh turns to grief when men look inside the ark and are struck down, and they too ask who can stand before this holy God. It is one movement: the LORD brings Himself home, and both pagan and Israelite learn that He is holy.
section.thesis: The LORD returns to His land by His own power, extracting tribute and confession from the Philistines and steering the cows Himself, yet He remains fearfully holy even to His own people, so that the story of the ark ends not with careless celebration but with the sober question, who can stand before this holy God, a question the gospel alone answers.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the Philistine plan and guilt offering, 6:1 to 9; the cows carry the ark home, 6:10 to 16; the gold tribute named, 6:17 to 18; and the holiness of God at Beth-shemesh and the ark received, 6:19 to 7:1.

units.label: The Philistine plan and guilt offering
units.note: return it to Him with a guilt offering

verses.text: When the ark of the LORD had been in the land of the Philistines seven months,
the Philistines summoned the priests and diviners, saying, “What shall we do with the ark of the LORD? Tell us how to send it back to its place.”

They replied, “If you return the ark of the God of Israel, do not send it away empty, but by all means return it to Him with a {{asham|guilt offering}}. Then you will be healed, and you will understand why His hand has not been lifted from you.”

“What guilt offering should we send back to Him?” asked the Philistines. “Five gold tumors and five gold rats,” they said, “according to the number of rulers of the Philistines, since the same plague has struck both you and your rulers.
Make images of your tumors and of the rats that are ravaging the land. Give {{kabod|glory}} to the God of Israel, and perhaps He will lift His hand from you and your gods and your land.
Why harden your hearts as the Egyptians and Pharaoh hardened theirs? When He afflicted them, did they not send the people out so they could go on their way?
Now, therefore, prepare one new cart with two milk cows that have never been yoked. Hitch the cows to the cart, but take their calves away and pen them up.
Take the ark of the LORD, set it on the cart, and in a chest beside it put the gold objects you are sending back to Him as a guilt offering. Then send the ark on its way,

but keep watching it. If it goes up the road to its homeland, toward Beth-shemesh, it is the LORD who has brought on us this great disaster. But if it does not, then we will know that it was not His hand that punished us and that it happened by chance.”

units.label: The cows carry the ark home
units.note: never straying to the right or to the left

verses.text: So the men did as instructed. They took two milk cows, hitched them to the cart, and penned up their calves.
Then they put the ark of the LORD on the cart, along with the chest containing the gold rats and the images of the tumors.

And the cows headed straight up the road toward Beth-shemesh, staying on that one highway and lowing as they went, never straying to the right or to the left. The rulers of the Philistines followed behind them to the border of Beth-shemesh.
Now the people of Beth-shemesh were harvesting wheat in the valley, and when they looked up and saw the ark, they were overjoyed at the sight.
The cart came to the field of Joshua of Beth-shemesh and stopped there near a large rock. The people chopped up the cart and offered the cows as a burnt offering to the LORD.

And the Levites took down the ark of the LORD and the chest containing the gold objects, and they placed them on the large rock. That day the men of Beth-shemesh offered burnt offerings and made sacrifices to the LORD.
And when the five rulers of the Philistines saw this, they returned to Ekron that same day.

units.label: The gold tribute named
units.note: a guilt offering to the LORD

verses.text: As a guilt offering to the LORD, the Philistines had sent back one gold tumor for each city: Ashdod, Gaza, Ashkelon, Gath, and Ekron.

The number of gold rats also corresponded to the number of Philistine cities belonging to the five rulers, the fortified cities and their outlying villages. And the large rock on which they placed the ark of the LORD stands to this day in the field of Joshua of Beth-shemesh.

units.label: The holiness of God and the ark received
units.note: Who can stand in the presence of the LORD, this holy God

verses.text: But God struck down some of the people of Beth-shemesh because they looked inside the ark of the LORD. He struck down seventy men, and the people mourned because the LORD had struck them with a great slaughter.
The men of Beth-shemesh asked, “Who can stand in the presence of the LORD, this {{qadosh|holy}} God? To whom should the ark go up from here?”
So they sent messengers to the people of Kiriath-jearim, saying, “The Philistines have returned the ark of the LORD. Come down and take it up with you.”
Then the men of Kiriath-jearim came for the ark of the LORD and took it into Abinadab’s house on the hill. And they consecrated his son Eleazar to guard the ark of the LORD.

asham.gloss: Guilt offering, reparation. In the law it made amends for wrong done to the LORD, restoring what was owed. Strikingly, the pagan Philistines are told to send an asham of gold, acknowledging their guilt before Israel's God and paying reparation for having offended Him. Even the nations sense that this God must be answered with an offering for guilt.

kabod.gloss: Glory, weight, honor. The Philistine priests urge, give glory to the God of Israel, that is, acknowledge His weight and honor as God. The very glory that departed from faithless Israel in chapter 4 is now to be confessed by pagans; giving God glory means bowing to His true greatness rather than resisting His hand.

qadosh.gloss: Holy, set apart, wholly other. When men die for peering into the ark, the survivors cry, who can stand before the LORD, this holy God? Holiness is not mere purity but God's awesome, unapproachable otherness. The question exposes the deepest human problem, how sinful people can live in the presence of a holy God, and it waits for the gospel's answer.

basic.context: After seven months of plague, the Philistines want the ark gone, but they want to be sure it really is Israel's God who has afflicted them. Their priests design a test: put the ark on a new cart with a gold guilt offering, hitch up two nursing cows, and let them loose. If the cows leave their calves and go straight to Israel, against every natural instinct, then it was the LORD. They do exactly that, lowing all the way, never turning aside. The people of Beth-shemesh rejoice, but then some look inside the ark and are struck down, and they ask, who can stand before this holy God? The ark is sent on to Kiriath-jearim. The question is: God is coming home in power, but how can sinful people live near such holiness?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative that joins God's sovereign power to His fearful holiness. The cows going straight home is a quiet miracle proving God's hand; the death at Beth-shemesh warns that even His own people cannot treat Him casually. We should not read the guilt offering as endorsing Philistine divination, nor use the deaths to make God seem arbitrary; the point is that God is really God, powerful and holy, and must be approached on His terms. The chapter leaves us asking a question only Christ answers.
basic.originalLanguages: The Philistines send an asham, a guilt offering, admitting their debt to Israel's God, and are urged to give Him kabod, glory, to acknowledge His weight. Yet the story ends on the word qadosh, holy: when men die for looking into the ark, the survivors cry, who can stand before the LORD, this holy God? Holiness is God's awesome otherness, and the question hangs unanswered until the gospel.
basic.typology: The guilt offering that even pagans knew must be paid points to the true guilt offering, Christ, who bears the reparation for sin. The question who can stand before this holy God finds its answer in the Mediator who makes access possible. And the ark coming home in power, yet dangerous to approach, foreshadows the holy God who draws near in Christ, in whom sinners may at last stand.
basic.christ: The whole ark narrative ends with an unanswered cry: who can stand before this holy God? Isaiah would ask it, and every awakened conscience asks it still. The gospel is the answer. God's holiness that struck men at Beth-shemesh is satisfied in Christ, the true guilt offering, who bears our reparation, and who, as our great High Priest, brings us near. Because of Him we may come with confidence to the throne of the holy God, not struck down but welcomed, our guilt paid, our access secured.
basic.god: God is sovereign, bending even the instincts of animals to bring Himself home and to leave the Philistines no excuse. He is honored by the nations, who must give Him glory and pay for their guilt. And He is holy, so holy that irreverence toward Him is deadly, even among His own people. He is not a tame God to be handled lightly, but the living, holy LORD who must be approached His way.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 6 records the same terror before the holy God, undone by atonement. Leviticus 5 sets out the guilt offering the Philistines unknowingly echo. Hebrews 4 invites us to approach the throne of grace with confidence through Christ. And 2 Samuel 6 shows the danger of handling the ark carelessly again.
basic.oneStory: The holy God brings Himself home in power, extracting confession from the nations, yet His holiness leaves even Israel asking how anyone can stand before Him. That question drives the whole story forward to Christ, the true guilt offering and High Priest, in whom the holy God draws near and sinful people are at last made able to stand.

context.literary: The resolution of the ark narrative, chapters 4 to 7. The mood swings from Philistine dread to Israelite joy and then to sober fear, ending with a question that hangs over the whole account. The unanswered question about standing before the holy God prepares for Samuel's call to wholehearted repentance in the next movement.
context.historical: Guilt offerings and votive gold images were common ancient responses to a god's anger; sending back plunder with reparation was a recognized way to appease an offended deity. Nursing cows would naturally return to their penned calves, so their steady march away from them, uphill toward Israel, was unmistakably supernatural. Looking into the ark violated the law that guarded God's holiness, Numbers 4.
context.canonical: The question who can stand before this holy God echoes through Scripture, in Isaiah's vision, in the Psalms, and supremely at the cross, where the holy God makes a way for sinners to stand. The guilt offering anticipates the sacrificial system's fulfillment in Christ, and the careless handling of the ark warns against the presumption that will resurface in David's day.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To prove by the returning cows that it was truly the LORD's hand afflicting the Philistines, and to confront both nations with God's holiness, ending the ark narrative on the unresolved question of how anyone can stand before Him.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes a Philistine test and a solemn judgment; it does not commend divination nor prescribe how to transport sacred objects. What endures is the truth of God's sovereign power, the reality of human guilt before Him, and the fearful holiness that only Christ enables sinners to approach.

debates.question: Why were the men of Beth-shemesh struck down for looking into the ark?

views.label: For violating the holiness that guarded God's presence
views.case: The law strictly forbade looking at or handling the holy things, Numbers 4:20; the men presumed upon God's holiness in curiosity or irreverence, and the judgment underscores that even Israel cannot treat the LORD casually.

views.label: To teach that God's nearness is not to be taken lightly
views.case: Coming so soon after the joy of the ark's return, the deaths warn that God's presence among His people is a gift to be revered, not a possession to be examined; His holiness is as real for Israel as for the Philistines.

debates.bearing: Either way the episode presses the question the men themselves ask, who can stand before this holy God, and drives the reader to the only answer, the atonement and mediation of Christ.

debates.question: What does it mean that even pagans are told to give glory and a guilt offering to Israel's God?

views.label: A recognition of the LORD's supremacy over the nations
views.case: The Philistine priests, chastened by the plagues, acknowledge that Israel's God is not merely a local deity but One whose hand they cannot resist, and whose honor and guilt they must address; the nations sense His universal claim.

views.label: A witness that all people are accountable to the true God
views.case: The instinct to give glory and pay for guilt, even among idolaters, testifies that God's rightful claim reaches every nation, foreshadowing the gospel's call to all peoples to glorify Him.

debates.bearing: Both readings show that the God of Israel is the God of all the earth, whose glory the nations must confess, a truth that opens toward the worldwide worship secured in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter moves through three weighty words. The Philistines are told to return the ark with an asham, a guilt offering, the very reparation the law of Leviticus prescribes for wrong done to the LORD; astonishingly, pagans sense they owe such a payment to Israel's God. They are urged to give Him kabod, glory, to acknowledge His weight and greatness, the same glory that had departed from faithless Israel in chapter 4 and that the nations must now confess. And the story lands on qadosh, holy: when curious men look into the ark and die, the survivors cry, who can stand before the LORD, this holy God? Qadosh is not merely moral purity but God's awesome, unapproachable otherness, and their question exposes the deepest human predicament, how sinful people can live in the presence of a holy God. The ark narrative closes not with a party but with a question, one the whole Bible will answer in Christ.

typology.type: The guilt offering even pagans knew must be paid
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true guilt offering who bears the reparation for sin, Isaiah 53:10 and Hebrews 9:14.

typology.type: The question, who can stand before this holy God
typology.fulfillment: Christ our High Priest, through whom sinners may come with confidence into God's holy presence, Hebrews 4:14-16.

typology.type: The holy ark coming home yet dangerous to approach
typology.fulfillment: The holy God drawing near in Christ, in whom the once unapproachable presence becomes accessible, Hebrews 10:19-22.

study.christ: The ark narrative ends with a cry that echoes through the whole Bible: who can stand before the LORD, this holy God? Isaiah asks it before the throne, and every conscience awakened to God's holiness asks it still. The gospel is the answer. The holiness that struck men dead at Beth-shemesh is not softened but satisfied in Christ, the true guilt offering, the asham foreshadowed here, who bears our reparation and cleanses our sin, Isaiah 53:10. As our great High Priest He does what no Israelite could do: He brings us near, so that we may approach the throne of the holy God with confidence, receiving mercy and grace, Hebrews 4:16. The answer to Beth-shemesh's question is a Person; in Him sinners no longer fall dead before holiness but are welcomed home.
study.god: This passage reveals a God of overwhelming sovereignty and fearful holiness. He is sovereign, bending the deepest instincts of nursing cows to carry His ark home and to leave the Philistines without excuse, so that they must confess it was His hand. He is honored by the nations, who give Him glory and pay a guilt offering, acknowledging His claim over all the earth. And He is holy, so holy that even His own people cannot peer into His presence and live; His nearness is a gift to be revered, not a possession to be handled. He is no tame or manageable deity but the living, holy LORD who comes in power and must be approached on His own terms, terms the gospel will graciously provide.

crossReferences.note: The prophet's terror before the holy God, undone only when atonement touches his lips, echoes the cry of Beth-shemesh.
The guilt offering for wrong done to the LORD, the asham the Philistines unknowingly imitate in gold.
Through Christ our High Priest we may approach the throne of the holy God with confidence, the answer to who can stand.
Uzzah struck for steadying the ark, a later reminder that God's holiness is not to be handled casually.

study.oneStory: The holy God brings Himself home in power, drawing confession and tribute from the nations, yet His holiness leaves even Israel asking how anyone can stand before Him. That unanswered question propels the whole story toward Christ, the true guilt offering and great High Priest, in whom the holy God draws near and sinful people are at last made able to stand. The ark's homecoming is a signpost pointing to the greater homecoming, when God dwells with His people forever in the Lamb who was slain.

quiz.q: How did the Philistines test whether it was really the LORD who had afflicted them?

options.text: They sent the ark on a cart pulled by nursing cows; if the cows left their calves and went straight to Israel, it was the LORD.
options.note: Verses 7 to 12 describe the test that proved God's hand by an unnatural, steady march.

options.text: They put the ark on a new cart and let it be driven toward Israel.
options.note: True in part, but the decisive sign was the nursing cows abandoning their calves against all instinct.

options.text: They asked Samuel to interpret a dream about the ark.
options.note: Samuel is not involved here; the test was the cart and cows.

options.text: They kept the ark and waited to see if the plague stopped.
options.note: The opposite; they sent the ark away to end the plague.

quiz.q: What did the Philistines send back with the ark, and why?

options.text: A guilt offering of gold tumors and rats, acknowledging their debt and giving glory to Israel's God.
options.note: Verses 3 to 5; even pagans sensed they must pay reparation and honor the LORD.

options.text: Gold images to appease the God of Israel.
options.note: True, but omits that it was specifically a guilt offering acknowledging their culpability.

options.text: Their finest weapons as a token of surrender.
options.note: They sent gold images of tumors and rats, not weapons.

options.text: Nothing; they sent the ark away empty.
options.note: The priests specifically warned not to send it away empty.

quiz.q: Why did the joy at Beth-shemesh turn to grief?

options.text: Men looked inside the ark and were struck down, so the people asked who can stand before this holy God.
options.note: Verses 19 to 20; God's holiness is deadly to the irreverent, even in Israel.

options.text: Some of the people of Beth-shemesh died after the ark arrived.
options.note: True, but omits the cause, looking into the ark, and the resulting question about God's holiness.

options.text: The Philistines attacked them and took the ark back.
options.note: The Philistines had gone home; the grief came from God's judgment, not an attack.

options.text: The ark was found to be empty and worthless.
options.note: The ark was holy; the deaths came from irreverence toward it.

quiz.q: What deep question does the ark narrative end on?

options.text: Who can stand before the LORD, this holy God, the question of how sinful people can live before holiness.
options.note: Verse 20 poses the question the gospel answers in Christ.

options.text: Where should the ark be kept now that it has returned.
options.note: That practical question is raised, but the weighty one is who can stand before the holy God.

options.text: Whether the Philistines would attack again.
options.note: The chapter's climax is God's holiness, not future Philistine raids.

options.text: How to make more gold offerings for the ark.
options.note: The question is about standing before a holy God, not about offerings of gold.

quiz.q: How does the question who can stand before this holy God find its answer?

options.text: In Christ, the true guilt offering and High Priest, through whom sinners may come near and stand before God.
options.note: Hebrews 4:16; Christ satisfies God's holiness and brings us near.

options.text: In the sacrifices and guilt offerings of the Old Testament law.
options.note: Those pointed the way, but the real answer is Christ, to whom they pointed.

options.text: By keeping a careful distance from God forever.
options.note: The gospel brings sinners near, rather than leaving them permanently at a distance.

options.text: There is no answer; no one can ever stand before God.
options.note: The gospel gives a real answer: in Christ we may stand before the holy God.

questions: Even pagans sensed they must give glory to God and pay for their guilt. Are there ways you resist God's rightful claim on your life that you need to acknowledge?
The cows went straight home against every instinct, proving God's hand. Where have you seen God's quiet providence steering events that you first thought were chance?
The people rejoiced until they mishandled the holy things. Do you ever treat God's presence, word, or worship casually, forgetting how holy He is?
Beth-shemesh asked, who can stand before this holy God? How does it change everything to know that in Christ you are welcomed rather than struck down?
The ark narrative ends with a question the gospel answers. How can holding together God's holiness and His welcome in Christ deepen both your reverence and your assurance?

translationNotes.note: Give glory to the God of Israel renders a call to honor the LORD, kabod; the same glory that departed in chapter 4 is here to be confessed even by pagans.
The source has an em dash before the fortified cities; rendered here as a comma. The large rock is called in Hebrew Abel-haggedolah, which the footnote notes may read great meadow.
The number struck down is textually uncertain; the BSB reads seventy men, while most Hebrew manuscripts read seventy men and fifty thousand men. The BSB follows a few manuscripts and Josephus.
Who can stand before the LORD, this holy God, uses qadosh, holy; the question voices the awe and dread of God's unapproachable presence.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Samuel 6:1-21 and 7:1, the movement spanning the chapter boundary. One em dash in 6:18 was converted to a comma; no em or en dashes remain anywhere in the file. Sermon status none: no sermon from the trusted cluster verified this session for this passage.
